review_summary: This unit supports procedural questions about layer-wise Jacobians, reusable error signals, and parameter-gradient shapes in vectorized backpropagation.

question_bank.question: Why is the Jacobian of an elementwise activation function diagonal?
choices: Each output coordinate depends only on the matching input coordinate.
Every output coordinate depends equally on every input coordinate.
The activation function has no derivative for positive inputs.
The weight matrix is always diagonal before the activation is applied.
question_bank.explanation: An elementwise activation applies the same scalar function separately to each coordinate. Changing one input coordinate only affects the corresponding output coordinate, so off-diagonal derivatives are zero.

question_bank.question: What is the practical purpose of defining the shared upstream gradient often called delta?
choices: It avoids recomputing the common chain-rule prefix when calculating multiple parameter gradients.
It forces all parameters in a layer to receive identical updates.
It replaces the local derivative of the activation function with a constant.
It converts a vector input into a one-hot representation.
question_bank.explanation: Several parameter derivatives share the same upstream part of the chain rule. Computing that shared error signal once and reusing it makes backpropagation more efficient and less repetitive.

question_bank.question: For an affine layer, why does the weight gradient have an outer-product pattern?
choices: Each weight connects one input coordinate to one output coordinate, so its gradient combines the local input with the upstream signal for that output.
Each weight depends on every input and every output equally, so the gradient must be a full covariance matrix.
Weights are updated only from the bias gradient, so inputs do not matter.
The activation Jacobian is dense, so every weight receives the same derivative.
question_bank.explanation: A single weight affects one output pre-activation through one input value. Its derivative therefore combines the upstream gradient for that output with the corresponding local input value, producing an outer-product-shaped matrix.
